Scenario.title: Maroochy Water Breach: Industrial Control System Compromise
Scenario.overview: Water and Wastewater Systems Complex A sophisticated cyberattack targeting a water treatment facility's SCADA systems, leveraging AI-driven reconnaissance, custom malware, and privilege escalation to disrupt critical infrastructure operations.
Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Open Source Intelligence, Script, Internet-facing assets, Target System Artifact, Windows Registry, Network Traffic, OS Process, Process, Domain Name Registrar
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: Artificial Intelligence, Script Execution (Mechanism), Delivery Method, Exploit TEE Vulnerability, Windows Registry Key Creation (Mechanism), Network Traffic Content (Mechanism), Process Injection, Process Creation (Mechanism), Reg.exe Utility, Pass the Hash, Exfiltration Over C2 Channel, Process Creation (Mechanism), Data Manipulation

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.description: The attacker uses artificial intelligence to analyze publicly available data about the water treatment facility's network architecture, vendor software, and personnel, identifying vulnerabilities in industrial control protocols.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.description: The threat actor develops custom scripts to automate the collection of SCADA system details, focusing on weaknesses in industrial control protocols used by the facility's legacy systems.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.description: The attacker compromises engineer credentials via spear-phishing emails containing malicious attachments, delivering stripped payloads to gain initial access to the facility's network.

stage.name: Execution
stage.capec: CAPEC-67: String Format Overflow in syslog()
stage.description: Malware is deployed to exploit vulnerabilities in outdated supervisory control systems, enabling arbitrary code execution on critical infrastructure components.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.description: Persistent access is established through scheduled tasks configured in the Windows Registry, ensuring continued control over key control nodes.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.capec: CAPEC-70: Try Common or Default Usernames and Passwords
stage.description: Elevated privileges are achieved by exploiting weak authentication mechanisms on remote maintenance interfaces, granting access to administrative controls.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.description: Malicious code is injected into legitimate SCADA monitoring processes to evade detection and maintain stealth during operations.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.capec: CAPEC-670: Software Development Tools Maliciously Altered
stage.description: Credentials are exfiltrated from operator workstations during lateral movement by manipulating software deployment tools to harvest login details.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.description: Network traffic is monitored using the Reg.exe utility to map the facility's internal network, identifying critical control systems and redundant pathways.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.capec: CAPEC-461: Web Services API Signature Forgery Leveraging Hash Function Extension Weakness
stage.description: The attacker moves laterally across the network using stolen credentials to access restricted control panels and escalate control over the infrastructure.

stage.name: Collection
stage.capec: CAPEC-24: Filter Failure through Buffer Overflow
stage.description: Operational data is collected and transmitted over a command-and-control channel to analyze system behavior prior to disruption.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.description: Sensitive configuration files are exfiltrated using process creation techniques to avoid detection by traditional network monitoring tools.

stage.name: Impact
stage.description: Critical systems are manipulated by altering input data to overload wastewater pumps and override safety protocols, resulting in sewage overflow incidents.

Scenario.summary: The Maroochy Water Breach demonstrates a multi-stage attack on industrial control systems, combining AI-driven reconnaissance, custom malware, and privilege escalation to disrupt critical infrastructure. The attack highlights vulnerabilities in legacy SCADA systems and poor security practices in water and wastewater management networks.
